title: Bâtarde, the hybrid Gothic cursive
subtitle: The fifteenth-century hybrid of textura and Gothic cursive
excerpt: Bâtarde is neither the formal upright textura nor the connected cursive chancery italic, but a hybrid that borrows the diamond-ended strokes and compressed letterforms of textura and adds the looped descenders and forward lean of cursive script. Widely used in fifteenth-century French and Flemish manuscripts and books, and the model for many early printed blackletter types.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), appendix on historical scripts, Johnston discusses the bâtarde and secretary hands in the historical survey. Pre-1928 facsimile plates of Burgundian court manuscripts (Bibliothèque nationale de France digitised collections) available in the public domain. The letterform analysis here draws on the Flemish bâtarde tradition documented in British Library manuscript collections.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hybrid Gothic-cursive script combining the compressed diamond letterforms of textura with the looped descenders and forward tilt of Gothic cursive. Widely used in fifteenth-century Burgundian court manuscripts.
The characteristic hairline-and-diamond entry stroke of the Gothic hands, made by starting a stroke at the upper right before pulling down and left, leaving a pointed entry serif.
A descender (p, g, y, q tails) that curves back upward in a closed or open loop rather than ending in a straight diagonal foot.

content.text: emerged in the Burgundian court in the early fifteenth century as scribes needed a script that combined the formality of textura with the speed of cursive. The word means 'bastard' in French, a polite acknowledgement that the script does not belong to either tradition. It was used for Books of Hours, court documents, and luxury manuscripts throughout the Flemish and French workshops.

items.name: Brause Bandzug nib (stiffer and wider than Mitchell; suits the heavier textura strokes)
items.substitutions: Mitchell Roundhand 1 or 2 for a lighter version

items.name: Iron-gall ink
items.substitutions: Walnut ink for a warmer-toned practice version

content.text: The nib angle for bâtarde is 35 to 40 degrees from the baseline, slightly steeper than textura (45 degrees) and shallower than italic. This angle gives thick downstrokes and thin hairline horizontals.

The x-height is 4 to 5 nib-widths, the same as textura. The letterforms lean very slightly forward, 2 to 3 degrees, rather than being fully upright. The compressed oval forms (o, c, e) are characteristic: they are narrow and pointed at top and bottom, not rounded.

The entry stroke of most bâtarde minuscule letters is a small diamond or wedge. To make it: position the nib at the upper right of the intended stroke start point, draw a tiny horizontal hairline to the left with the edge of the nib, then pivot and pull down into the main stroke. The result is a small angular foot at the head of each vertical. This replaces the square serif of formal textura with a more angular, slightly rough entry.
Stroke 1: make the diamond entry at cap-height. Pull a slightly curved right stroke down to the baseline, maintaining a slight bow to the left (the 'arch' of the a). End with a thin foot hairline.
Stroke 2: starting from the top of stroke 1, pull a comma-shaped stroke to form the bowl, curving left and down, ending at the mid-point of the baseline.
Stroke 3: close the bowl by pulling a short stroke left from the foot of stroke 2 to meet the bottom of stroke 1.

The p, g, y, and q descenders in bâtarde curve back upward in a loop, the end often touching or almost touching the main stem. This is the most characteristic difference from formal textura, where descenders end in a straight diagonal. The loop is made by pulling the descender stroke downward, then curving left and upward, bringing the pen across the bottom in a smooth hairline. The loop height should be about half the x-height.

items.symptom: The script looks like textura, not bâtarde
items.cause: Too upright; not enough forward lean; descenders are not looping.
items.fix: Add a 2 to 3 degree forward slant deliberately. Practice the g and p descenders separately before working full words.

items.symptom: The letterforms look clumsy rather than compressed
items.cause: The compressed oval has not been formed correctly; the o is too round.
items.fix: The bâtarde o is very narrow, almost as tall as it is wide, pointed at top and bottom. Re-work the o form before attempting connected text.

content.text: Bâtarde is the immediate ancestor of the printed blackletter types used in the earliest German and Dutch printed books. Studying it alongside
shows how the two traditions interacted and how type-founders resolved their differences.
